title: Hawaiian Electric Industries to Announce First Quarter 2026 Results May 8
content:
HONOLULU, April 06, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (HEI) (NYSE - HE) will announce its first quarter 2026 financial results on Friday, May 8 and conduct a webcast and conference call to discuss the results at 10:30 a.m. Hawaii time (4:30 p.m. Eastern time).

To listen to the conference call, dial 1-888-660-6377 (U.S.) or 1-929-203-0797 (international) and enter passcode 2393042. Parties may also access presentation materials and/or listen to the conference call by visiting the conference call link on HEI’s website at www.hei.com under "Investor Relations," sub-heading "News and Events – Events and Presentations."

A replay will be available online and via phone. The online replay will be available on HEI’s website about two hours after the event. An audio replay will also be available about two hours after the event through May 15. To access the audio replay, dial 1-800-770-2030 (U.S.) or 1-647-362-9199 (international) and enter passcode 2393042.

HEI and Hawaiian Electric Company, Inc. (Hawaiian Electric) intend to continue to use HEI’s website, www.hei.com, as a means of disclosing additional information; such disclosures will be included in the Investor Relations section of the website. Accordingly, investors should routinely monitor the Investor Relations section of HEI’s website, in addition to following HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s press releases, HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings and HEI’s public conference calls and webcasts. Investors may sign up to receive e-mail alerts via the Investor Relations section of the website. The information on HEI’s website is not incorporated by reference into this document or into HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s SEC filings unless, and except to the extent, specifically incorporated by reference.

Investors may also wish to refer to the Public Utilities Commission of the State of Hawaii (PUC) website at https://hpuc.my.site.com/cdms/s/ to review documents filed with, and issued by, the PUC. No information on the PUC website is incorporated by reference into this document or into HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s SEC filings.

About HEI

HEI provides the energy services that empower much of the economic and community activity of Hawaii. HEI’s sole operating company is its electric utility, Hawaiian Electric. Hawaiian Electric supplies power to approximately 95% of Hawaii’s population and is undertaking an ambitious effort to decarbonize its operations and the broader state economy, and modernize and harden the grid to ensure resilience and public safety. For more information, visit www.hei.com.

title: Stocks to watch after market: NVDA, FBIN,HE
content:
[glowing graph 2]
Jonathan Kitchen

Wall Street's major averages closed higher on Monday as oil prices slightly declined and investors continued to keep their eyes on the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500 [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SP500#hasComeFromMpArticle=false#source=section%3Amain_content%7Cbutton%3Abody_link%7Cfirst_level_url%3Anews]) ended +1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/COMP:IND]) closed +1.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DJI]) finished +0.8%.

STOCKS TO WATCH

NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]) 

Nvidia (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]) CEO Jensen Huang made waves at the company’s annual GTC event on Monday, doubling down on AI demand projections and unveiling plans to bring computing to space. “We saw $500B in GPU demand last year for Blackwell and Rubin,” Huang said during his keynote. “I’m here to tell you that right here where I stand, one year after the last GTC, I see through 2027 at least $1T.”

The company announced the Space-1 Vera Rubin Module, which offers 25 times more AI compute than the H100 GPU, as part of its strategy to power next-generation space-based data centers. Intel (INTC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/INTC]) is providing Xeon 6 processors for NVIDIA DGX Rubin NVL8 systems. Huang also highlighted partnerships with cloud providers including Amazon Web Services (AMZN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMZN]), Oracle (ORCL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ORCL]), Google (GOOGL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GOOGL]), and Microsoft (MSFT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MSFT]), while space-tech collaborators like Aetherflux, Axiom Space, Kepler Communications, and Starcloud are already using Nvidia’s platforms for orbital and ground environments.

FORTUNE BRANDS INNOVATIONS (FBIN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FBIN])

Fortune Brands Innovations (FBIN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FBIN]) announced a sweeping leadership overhaul on Monday, appointing David Barry as interim CEO effective immediately while launching a search for a permanent successor. The company said Amit Banati will no longer assume the CEO role in May and has stepped down from the board, while former CEO Nicholas Fink also accelerated his previously planned departure. In a separate move, CFO Jonathan Baksht stepped down with immediate effect, and Ashley George was named interim CFO during the search for a permanent replacement. The board also appointed Ed Garden, founding partner and CEO of Garden Investments, as a director under a cooperation agreement with the investment firm.

HAWAIIAN ELECTRIC (HE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HE])

Hawaiian Electric (HE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HE]) announced Monday that the U.S. District Court for the District of Hawaii granted preliminary approval for a derivative settlement agreement between the company and its shareholders, which will resolve all pending derivative lawsuits. The settlement provides for a $100M payment to resolve claims asserted in derivative actions pending in federal and state courts in Hawaii, as well as a related case in California. The agreement, finalized on December 31, includes no admission of liability, and defendants and related parties will receive a full release of all claims. The court has scheduled May 28 as the hearing date for final approval of the settlement.

title: Hawaiian Electric Industries Q4 Earnings Call Highlights
content:
Hawaiian Electric Industries logo

Key Points

The company said the Maui wildfire tort settlements are in the “home stretch” after the Hawaii Supreme Court blocked insurer intervention, but a remaining appeal on earlier summary judgment must be resolved before the first $479 million payment can be made, now expected in the second half of 2026; management plans to fund settlement payments with a mix of debt and likely convertible debt depending on market conditions. Hawaiian Electric reported a full-year 2025 net income of $123.1 million (vs. a ~$1.4 billion loss in 2024) and core net income of $149.3 million, while holding-company and utility liquidity stood at about $16 million and $486 million of unrestricted cash, respectively, plus significant available capacity under credit and ATM facilities. Management expects a higher capital spending cycle—utility capex of $550–700 million in 2026 rising to $600–850 million by 2028—and is pursuing regulatory steps including a joint PBR rebasing proposal due March 6 and potential securitization to finance wildfire-safety and resilience investments. Interested in Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc.? Here are five stocks we like better.

End the Year Strong With These 3 Comeback Champions

Hawaiian Electric Industries (NYSE:HE) used its fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 earnings call to highlight progress on litigation tied to the 2023 Maui wildfires, ongoing regulatory and legislative initiatives related to wildfire risk, and plans for a higher capital spending cycle in coming years.

Maui wildfire settlement process and related litigation updates

CEO Scott Seu said the company continued executing priorities it outlined after the 2023 Maui wildfires, including advancing the Maui wildfire tort settlement and implementing wildfire safety improvements intended to reduce the risk of ignition from utility equipment.

→ Diamondback Sees Resilient Demand Despite Cautious Guidance

3 Energy Stocks That Could Rally If the Oil Bears Are Wrong

On the settlement process, Seu said the company is working through remaining contingencies to payment and characterized the effort as being in the “home stretch,” with the “only remaining steps” described as resolving all outstanding appeals.

Management outlined several recent court developments:

In late December, the Maui Circuit Court granted the company’s motion for summary judgment on subrogation insurers’ direct claims. In January, the court granted final approval of the class settlement agreement and provided a good faith settlement determination. On Feb. 10, the Hawaii Supreme Court affirmed the lower court’s denial of subrogation insurers’ motion to intervene in the class settlement process. Seu said the decision “ends the insurer’s efforts to derail the class settlement” and “moves us one step closer toward final court approval of the settlement agreements.”

Story Continues

→ AI Is Separating Software Winners From Losers, 2 Experts Explain

ExxonMobil: Limited Risk and Lots of Reward With This Oil Play

During the Q&A, Seu said the remaining item is an appeal filed in January related to the earlier summary judgment that dismissed insurers’ direct claims. He noted there had been no briefing scheduled yet and declined to speculate on how the Hawaii Supreme Court will act, while adding that prior decisions by the circuit court and the Hawaii Supreme Court “have been very supportive of the settlements.”

Separately, the company said it finalized settlements resolving both the shareholder class action and shareholder derivative lawsuits tied to the Maui wildfires. Seu said binding term sheets were signed in early November, and the agreements were finalized and executed in late December and early January. Management said the company’s obligations under those agreements are fully funded by insurance proceeds.

2025 financial results and liquidity

→ NVIDIA’s AI Boom Isn’t Slowing After Blowout Q4

Executive Vice President and CFO Scott DeGatto reported full-year 2025 net income of $123.1 million, or $0.71 per share, compared to a net loss of approximately $1.4 billion in 2024.

Results included $16.5 million of pre-tax Maui wildfire-related expenses, net of insurance recoveries and deferrals, with approximately $12.6 million recorded at the utility. Results also included $12.4 million of losses related to the strategic review of Pacific Current. Excluding these items, which the company refers to as non-core, consolidated core net income was $149.3 million, or $0.86 per share, compared with core income from continuing operations of $124.3 million, or $0.98 per share, in 2024.

Utility core net income was $177.5 million in 2025 versus $180.7 million in 2024. DeGatto attributed the decline to higher operations and maintenance expenses (primarily from previously deferred consulting and legal fees), higher interest expense, higher depreciation, and the recognition of tax credit benefits in the prior year.

Holding company core net loss improved to $28.2 million from $56.4 million in 2024. DeGatto said the change was driven by lower interest expense following the retirement of holding company debt in April and higher interest income from cash held on the balance sheet for the first settlement payment.

As of the end of the fourth quarter, management said the holding company and the utility had approximately $16 million and $486 million of unrestricted cash, respectively. DeGatto added that the holding company had roughly $530 million in combined liquidity under its at-the-market (ATM) program and credit facility capacity, while the utility had approximately $540 million of liquidity available under its accounts receivable facility and credit facility capacity.

DeGatto also said Hawaiian Electric’s board approved a $10 million quarterly dividend to HEI for the fourth quarter of 2025, consistent with the prior quarter.

Settlement funding and capital spending outlook

The company said there were no changes to its settlement financing plan from what it communicated previously. DeGatto said the company expects to fund the second settlement payment with debt and/or convertible debt, and that payments thereafter are expected to be funded with a mix of debt and equity depending on market conditions.

DeGatto said the first $479 million settlement payment cannot be made until outstanding appeals are resolved, and the company now expects that payment to be made in the second half of 2026.

In response to an analyst question, DeGatto said the company is currently leaning toward using convertible debt for the “relevering” at HEI, though he said plans could change with market conditions. He also said the company does not anticipate taking action on that financing until after the settlement is approved.

On capital spending, management reiterated expected utility capex of $550 million to $700 million in 2026. The company also expects capex to rise further to $600 million to $800 million in 2027 and $600 million to $850 million in 2028, subject to additional Public Utilities Commission (PUC) approvals and further resource adequacy initiatives and analysis.

Regulatory and legislative developments: PBR, wildfire legislation, and securitization

Seu discussed the company’s performance-based regulation (PBR) framework and ongoing work toward a second multi-year rate period. He said HEI is pursuing an alternative rate rebasing process intended to reset rates without the time and cost of a traditional rate case, consistent with PBR principles and a stakeholder-driven approach.

Management said it plans to submit a joint rebasing proposal with stakeholder Ulupono Initiative by March 6. Utility regulatory executive Joe Viola said the commission is expected to take about 30 days to review for administrative completeness before issuing an order to proceed, and that the company would pivot to a traditional 2027 test year rate case if the rebasing proposal were denied.

Management also pointed to potential improvements under the PBR framework to be addressed in a process the PUC has designated as PBR phase 6, including the annual inflationary adjustment and performance incentive mechanisms (PIMs). In Q&A, management described focus areas including a “true-up mechanism” for the inflationary adjustment factor, PIM design so targets and outcomes are within the company’s control, and potentially reducing the number of PIMs. Management also referenced expanding the scope of the Exceptional Project Recovery Mechanism (EPRM).

On wildfire legislation, Seu referenced the “historic” wildfire law signed in July and said the PUC’s Wildfire Fund study was completed at the end of December as a first step. He said work continues to establish a liability cap through a PUC rulemaking process expected to take 18 to 24 months, with details around the Wildfire Fund to be established sometime thereafter. Viola added that in 2026 the key milestones are tied to the PUC rulemaking process and said there is nothing expected to be teed up before the legislature this year.

Seu also said the PUC approved the utility’s three-year wildfire safety strategy in late December and concluded the strategy can be expected to reduce wildfire risk. The company said it has achieved many operational objectives in the strategy ahead of schedule and will submit its next update to the PUC in April.

Looking to financing, Seu said the company is pursuing low-cost financing options to reduce customer impacts from safety and resilience investments, including a planned request in coming months to finance wildfire safety strategy capex and other infrastructure resilience costs via securitization, which he described as typically the lowest cost of capital available for such investments.

Renewables, customer affordability, and leadership transition

Seu said the utility reached a 37% renewable portfolio standard (RPS) in 2025 and remains on track to meet the statutory 40% by 2030 requirement. He also said customer bills remained stable in 2025 despite investments in wildfire safety and resilience, and that the utility provided more than $1 million in payment assistance to working families.

The company also announced an executive transition. Seu said DeGatto’s term as HEI CFO expires April 1, and DeGatto will resign effective April 2. Paul Ito, currently treasurer and CFO of Hawaiian Electric, will become HEI CFO effective April 2, 2026. Seu said DeGatto will continue to support the company as a consultant.

About Hawaiian Electric Industries (NYSE:HE)

Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc is a diversified holding company operating in the energy and financial services sectors in the state of Hawaii. Its principal subsidiary, Hawaiian Electric Company, provides generation, transmission, distribution and customer service to the island of Oahu, while its Maui Electric and Hawaii Electric Light Company subsidiaries serve Maui, Molokai, Lanai and Hawaii Island. The roots of the electric utility business trace back to 1891 when service first commenced in Honolulu.

Through its subsidiary Hawaii Gas, HEI extends its energy portfolio to include the distribution of natural gas and propane, supporting residential, commercial and industrial customers across the islands.

Hawaiian Electric Industries Inc (NYSE:HE) achieved a 37% renewable portfolio standard in 2025, on track to meet the 40% statutory requirement by 2030. The utility maintained stable customer bills in 2025 despite significant investments in wildfire safety and resilience. The company successfully issued $500 million in utility debt and increased its revolver to $600 million, enhancing financial flexibility and liquidity.

Negative Points

Hawaiian Electric Industries Inc (NYSE:HE) faced a net income decrease in 2025 compared to 2024, driven by higher operational and maintenance expenses, interest expenses, and depreciation. The company recorded $16.5 million in pre-tax Maui wildfire-related expenses, impacting financial results. There is ongoing uncertainty regarding the resolution of outstanding appeals related to the Maui wildfire settlement, which could delay the first settlement payment. The company anticipates significant capital expenditures in the coming years, with 2026 CapEx projected at $550 million to $700 million, increasing further in 2027 and 2028. The transition of the CFO role from Scott DeGhetto to Paul Ito may present challenges during the leadership change.

Story Continues

Q & A Highlights

Q: What are the chances that the Hawaii Supreme Court will take up the latest appeal by the insurers regarding the Maui wildfire settlement? A: Scott Seu, President and CEO, stated that while he cannot predict the court's actions, all previous decisions by the Circuit Court and the State Supreme Court have been supportive of the settlements. The appeal is the last step, and there is optimism about a favorable outcome.

Q: What are the plans for financing the second settlement payment, and will it be similar to the first settlement capital raise? A: Scott DeGhetto, Executive Vice President and CFO, mentioned that the plan is to use either debt or convertible debt, with a current preference for convertible debt based on market conditions. The financing will occur after the settlement is approved, unlike the first payment, which was raised well in advance.

Q: How does Hawaiian Electric plan to finance its capital program, and will the ATM program be utilized? A: Scott DeGhetto explained that the ATM program is available and may be used opportunistically depending on market conditions. Paul Ito, the incoming CFO, will provide further insights on utility financing strategies.

Q: What are the key elements of the upcoming March 6 joint proposal for PBR rebasing? A: Scott Seu highlighted that the proposal will address the inflationary adjustment factor, introduce a true-up mechanism, and refine performance incentive mechanisms (PIMs) to ensure they are achievable and impactful. The proposal aims to streamline and improve the regulatory framework.

Q: What are the expected milestones for the wildfire liability cap and fund in 2026? A: Scott Seu outlined that the PUC's rule-making process for the liability cap is expected to take 18 to 24 months, starting in early 2026. The process involves data gathering, public comments, and final rule issuance, with no immediate legislative actions anticipated this year.

title: Hawaiian Electric signals $550M–$700M capex for 2026 as wildfire settlements near final approval
content:
Earnings Call Insights: Hawaiian Electric Industries (HE) Q4 2025

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* Scott W. Seu, President and CEO, opened the call by highlighting progress on priorities since the Maui wildfires in 2023, including advancing the Maui wildfire tort settlement, legislative measures for wildfire risk, and implementing safety improvements that "have reduced the risk of ignition from utility equipment and laying the groundwork for a successful second multiyear rate period under our performance-based regulation, or PBR framework." 
 	* Seu stated, "In sum, we've continued to work through the administrative steps required to see the settlement through to completion and trigger our first payment." He noted that class settlement agreements have received court approvals, and a recent Hawaii Supreme Court decision ended insurer efforts to intervene, moving the company closer to final court approval and triggering the first payment.
 	* Seu confirmed finalization of settlements resolving both the shareholder class action and shareholder derivative lawsuits related to the Maui wildfires, with obligations "fully funded by insurance proceeds."
 	* Seu highlighted that the Public Utilities Commission (PUC) approved the utility's 3-year wildfire safety strategy in December, and the company achieved many objectives ahead of schedule. "We'll continue rapidly advancing the strategy as we progress through 2026."
 	* Key financial flexibility measures included a $500 million utility debt issuance, upsizing the revolver to $600 million, and maintaining stable customer bills despite investments in wildfire safety.
 	* Strategic priorities for 2026 involve resolving remaining conditions for the tort litigation settlement and pursuing an alternative process for rate rebasing, with a joint proposal submission planned by March 6.
 	* Seu confirmed an executive transition: "Scott DeGhetto's term as HEI's CFO expires on April 1... Paul Ito, the current Treasurer and CFO of Hawaiian Electric will resume his prior role as HEI's CFO, effective April 2, 2026."
 	* Scott Deghetto, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "For the full year 2025, we generated net income of $123.1 million or $0.71 per share compared to a net loss of approximately $1.4 billion in 2024... consolidated core net income was $149.3 million or $0.86 per share."

OUTLOOK

 	* Management reiterated that "we still expect 2026 CapEx of $550 million to $700 million" and projected increases for 2027 and 2028, subject to PUC approvals and resource adequacy reviews.
 	* Seu stated, "In 2026, we'll continue to execute on our key objectives of advancing the tort settlement and our rate rebasing process while implementing the wildfire risk reduction measures outlined in our wildfire safety strategy."
 	* The company is planning to submit a request for securitization to finance wildfire safety CapEx and infrastructure resilience costs, focusing on minimizing customer impact.
 	* Management emphasized that "affordability remains a central focus as we look ahead toward the commencement of the second multiyear rate period under PBR."

FINANCIAL RESULTS

 	* Deghetto reported, "The results include $16.5 million of pretax Maui wildfire-related expenses net of insurance recoveries and deferrals... Results for the year also include $12.4 million of losses related to the strategic review of Pacific Current."
 	* Utility core net income was $177.5 million, while holding company core net loss was $28.2 million, with improved loss figures driven by lower interest expense and increased interest income from cash held for the settlement payment.
 	* As of Q4, the holding company and utility had $16 million and $486 million of unrestricted cash on hand, respectively, and significant liquidity through credit facilities.
 	* The Board approved a $10 million quarterly dividend to HEI for Q4 2025.
 	* Deghetto confirmed no changes to settlement financing plans, with the first $479 million payment expected in the second half of 2026 after appeals are resolved.

Q&A

 	* Michael Lonegan, Barclays: Asked about the likelihood of the Hawaii Supreme Court hearing the insurers’ appeal and settlement payment timing. Seu replied, "There's been no briefing scheduled yet on this appeal that was just filed in January... this is the last step... all decisions by the Circuit Court and the State Supreme Court have been very supportive of the settlements."
 	* Lonegan queried settlement financing preferences. Deghetto answered, "It will be a relevering at HEI either through debt or convertible debt. Right now, based upon market conditions, I would say we're leaning more towards convertible debt... but that certainly can change."
 	* Lonegan followed up on use of the ATM program for capital needs. Deghetto: "We may use it. We may not. It just again, depends on market conditions, but that's always an option for us."
 	* Lonegan also asked about the sale of the remaining stake in American Savings Bank. Deghetto: "We still intend to divest that remaining 9.9% in calendar year 2026."
 	* Jamieson Ward, Jefferies: Asked about the upcoming March 6 joint proposal for PBR rebasing. Seu listed priorities as "inflationary adjustment factor, having a true-up mechanism... Looking at the PIMS and making sure the PIMS are nice and tight in terms of our ability to actually influence outcomes."
 	* Ward inquired about risk triggers for a pivot to a traditional rate case. Joe Viola, SVP, responded, "The only thing that would cause us to pivot to a 2027 test year rate case is if that rate basing falls over denied."
 	* Ward asked about PIM redesign for more meaningful incentives. Viola: "We want to make sure that the targets are reasonably set and the means to achieve them are reasonably within our control."
 	* Ward questioned 2026 milestones for wildfire fund and liability cap. Seu explained details of the 18-24 month PUC rule-making process, with no major legislative action expected in 2026.

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious tone, mainly focusing on appeals, financing strategies, and regulatory process milestones, with questions probing for clarity on timing and risk around settlements, capital raises, and regulatory outcomes.
 	* Management's prepared remarks were confident and optimistic, but in response to analyst questions, showed caution regarding legal processes and financing flexibility. Seu used measured language: "I don't want to get ahead of our Hawaii Supreme Court" and "maybe I'll stop my comments there."
 	* Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence level has increased regarding settlement progress, while analysts remain focused on execution risks and regulatory clarity.

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* The current quarter saw a significant update on settlement progress, with final court approvals and clear next steps, compared to the previous quarter’s focus on pending court hearings and administrative steps.
 	* CapEx guidance for 2026 ($550 million to $700 million) was reiterated, in line with last quarter, but with additional detail on longer-term increases and financing methods.
 	* Previous quarter discussions centered more on implementing wildfire safety strategy and early-stage legislative and regulatory milestones, while this quarter emphasized settlement finalization and PBR rebasing process details.
 	* Analysts' focus shifted from uncertainties in the settlement timeline and CapEx guidance to more granular questions about regulatory mechanisms, financing preferences, and execution risk management.
 	* Management’s tone remains optimistic but is more forward-looking, especially regarding the legal resolution and regulatory process, while analysts maintain a probing, risk-focused approach.

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Management highlighted the need for final resolution of outstanding appeals before the $479 million settlement payment can be made.
 	* Ongoing challenges include successful execution of the wildfire safety strategy, regulatory approval of rate rebasing, and securing low-cost financing for CapEx.
 	* The company faces risk from further legal proceedings, regulatory delays, and capital market conditions affecting financing plans.
 	* Analysts raised concerns about appeal outcomes, timing of capital raises, and the impact of regulatory processes on earnings visibility.

FINAL TAKEAWAY

Hawaiian Electric Industries reported substantial progress toward finalizing the Maui wildfire tort settlements, advanced wildfire safety investments, and maintained stable customer bills despite significant capital outlays. The company reiterated its 2026 CapEx guidance of $550 million to $700 million and outlined further regulatory and financing milestones ahead, including a joint proposal on rate rebasing and a planned CFO transition. Management emphasized that successful litigation resolution and prudent capital management will remain top priorities as the company continues its strategic transformation in the face of evolving risks and regulatory demands.

title: HEI Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Results
content:
Regulators Approved Utility’s Enhanced Wildfire Safety Strategy in December; Strategy Calls for Deploying New Technology, Fortifying Infrastructure, Minimizing Fire Hazards and Expanding Community Partnerships to Significantly Reduce Wildfire Risk PUC’s Wildfire Fund Study, Completed in December, Represents Positive First Step in Implementing Hawaii’s Historic Wildfire Legislation Signed Into Law in July Wildfire Tort Litigation Settlement Nearing Final Court Approval Core Operations Performed Well in 2025, As Utility Continued to Make Critical Investments to Support Safe, Reliable and Resilient Electricity Delivery for Our Communities Strong Balance Sheet and Liquidity Position Ensure Our Ability to Serve and Invest in Our Communities for the Long Term Utility Achieved a 37% Renewable Portfolio Standard in 2025, Accelerating Progress Toward 2030’s 40% Statutory Milestone Typical Residential Bill Stable in 2025. Utility Providing $1 Million in Payment Assistance to Customers

HONOLULU, February 27, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (NYSE - HE) (HEI) today reported net income for the full year 2025 of $123 million, or $0.71 per share, compared to a net loss of $1,426 million, or $11.23 per share in 2024. Excluding Maui wildfire-related expenses and expenses taken in connection with the review of strategic options for Pacific Current, Core1 income from continuing operations2 was $149 million, or $0.86 per share, compared to $124 million, or $0.98 per share in 20243.

Fourth quarter 2025 net income was $40 million, or $0.23 per share, compared to a net loss of $68 million, or $0.40 per share in 2024. Core income from continuing operations was $41 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $35 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.

"Over the past year, we continued to take actions to ensure that we’re best positioned to serve the communities in which we operate for the long term. We made critical progress on initiatives we’ve worked toward for much of the last three years: advancing the Maui wildfire tort settlements toward final court approval, pursuing legislative measures that enable us to better serve our communities as we deal with the risk of wildfires, implementing wildfire safety improvements, and laying the groundwork for a successful second multi-year rate period under our Performance Based Regulation framework," said Scott Seu, HEI president and CEO.

"Our core utility business continued to perform well in 2025, operating efficiently and maintaining financial strength while making the critical investments necessary to ensure safe, reliable and resilient service to our communities. Affordability has been a central focus of these efforts, with rates stable compared to a year ago, and the utility offering financial assistance to working families while providing more options for customers to manage their bills. We also saw the utility reaching a 37% renewable portfolio standard in 2025, putting us on track to meet the 40% RPS by 2030 statutory requirement. HEI continues to be well-positioned to execute our commitment to a stronger, more resilient and more financially healthy future."

Story Continues

____________________ Note: Throughout this release, per share values are calculated based on diluted shares. 1  Measures described as "Core" for the periods in this news release are non-GAAP measures which exclude Maui wildfire-related costs and expenses taken in connection with the strategic review of Pacific Current. See the "Explanation of HEI’s Use of Certain Unaudited Non-GAAP Measures" and the related GAAP reconciliation at the end of this release. 2  Income from continuing operations excludes 2024 net income from American Savings Bank (ASB). 3  Decrease in per share amount due to larger share count following September 2024 equity issuance.

HAWAIIAN ELECTRIC COMPANY (HAWAIIAN ELECTRIC) EARNINGS

Full Year Results:

Hawaiian Electric’s net income for the full year 2025 was $168 million compared to a net loss of $1,226 million for the full year 2024, with the increase primarily driven by the following pre-tax items (among others):

The $1,875 million loss recorded in 2024 due to the accrual of estimated wildfire liabilities related to tort-related legal claims and cross claims (net of insurance recoveries); $21 million in higher revenues, primarily from the annual revenue adjustment mechanism; and $9 million impact from better heat rate performance.

These items were partially offset by (among others):

$11 million in higher O&M, driven by $10 million in previously deferred legal and consulting fees; $10 million in higher interest expense; and $5 million of higher depreciation expense.

Hawaiian Electric’s Core net income for 2025 was $178 million compared to $181 million in 2024. Pre-tax wildfire-related expenses of $42 million were partially offset by approximately $28 million of costs deferred pursuant to the Public Utilities Commission’s decision allowing Hawaiian Electric to defer these costs.

Fourth Quarter Results:

Hawaiian Electric’s net income for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $44 million compared to net income of $46 million in the fourth quarter of 2024, with the decrease primarily driven by the following pre-tax items (among others):

$7 million in higher interest expense; $1 million in higher O&M expenses; and $1 million in higher depreciation expense.

These items were partially offset by (among others):

$5 million in higher revenues, primarily from the annual revenue adjustment mechanism; and $3 million in higher interest income.

Hawaiian Electric’s Core net income for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $46 million compared to $49 million in the same quarter last year. Pre-tax wildfire-related expenses of $9 million were partially offset by approximately $6 million of costs deferred pursuant to the Public Utilities Commission’s decision allowing Hawaiian Electric to defer these costs.

HOLDING AND OTHER COMPANIES

The holding and other companies’ net loss was $45 million in 2025 compared to $96 million in 2024. The lower net loss for the year was primarily due to expenses taken in 2024 related to strategic reviews, and lower Maui wildfire expenses. Core net loss for the year was $28 million compared to $56 million in 2024, primarily due to lower interest expense following the retirement of holding company debt with ASB sale proceeds, and higher interest income. The fourth quarter net loss was $5 million in 2025 compared to $17 million in 2024. Core net loss for the quarter was $4 million compared to $14 million in 2024.

title: Earnings week ahead: NVDA, CRM, HD, BIDU, LOW, DELL, SNOW, AMC, ZM, and more
content:
[earnings]
MCCAIG

The last week of February brings another packed stretch of earnings, with a mix of megacap technology leaders, consumer bellwethers, energy producers, financial firms, and high-growth disruptors set to report.

The lineup spans AI heavyweights, real estate income plays, cloud software providers, and cyclical industrial names, offering investors a broad read on enterprise spending, housing-linked demand, digital advertising, and commodity trends as the quarter progresses.

Technology and AI will dominate the spotlight with updates from NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]), Salesforce (CRM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CRM]), Snowflake (SNOW [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SNOW]), The Trade Desk (TTD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TTD]), Zoom Communications (ZM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ZM]), Zscaler (ZS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ZS]), C3.ai [https://C3.ai] (AI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AI]), IonQ (IONQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IONQ]), D-Wave Quantum (QBTS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/QBTS]), Dell Technologies (DELL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DELL]), HP (HPQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HPQ]), Workday (WDAY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/WDAY]), Intuit (INTU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/INTU]), CoreWeave (CRWV [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CRWV]), and SoundHound AI (SOUN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SOUN]).

Consumer, retail, and payments trends will be reflected through results from The Home Depot (HD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HD]), Lowe's (LOW [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LOW]), Block (XYZ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XYZ]), MercadoLibre (MELI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MELI]), Baidu (BIDU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BIDU]), Lucid Group (LCID [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LCID]), AMC Entertainment (AMC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMC]), Hims & Hers Health (HIMS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HIMS]), and Teladoc Health (TDOC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TDOC]).

Energy, infrastructure, and materials updates from ONEOK (OKE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OKE]), Dominion Energy (D [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/D]), Cheniere Energy (LNG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LNG]), Diamondback Energy (FANG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FANG]), EOG Resources (EOG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/EOG]), MP Materials (MP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MP]), and First Solar (FSLR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FSLR]) will offer signals on commodity demand, pricing, and capital discipline.

Real estate, income, and financially linked names, including Realty Income (O [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/O]), VICI Properties (VICI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VICI]), American Tower (AMT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMT]), Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IIPR]), EPR Properties (EPR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/EPR]), Main Street Capital (MAIN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MAIN]), and Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TD]), will add insight into financing conditions, leasing demand, and credit trends.

Altogether, the week’s reports should provide a wide-angle view on AI spending momentum, consumer resilience, capital investment, and interest-rate sensitivity as markets head toward the final stretch of the quarter.

Below is a rundown of major quarterly updates anticipated in the week of February 23 to February 27:

MONDAY, FEBRUARY 23

DOMINION ENERGY (D [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/D])

Dominion Energy (D [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/D]) is set to report Q4 earnings before Monday’s market open, with analysts expecting roughly a 16% Y/Y increase in profits on revenue growth of more than 9%.

The utility was recently in focus after securing a court ruling [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4539989-dominion-wins-court-ok-to-resume-virginia-offshore-wind-project-stopped-by-trump] allowing it to resume work on its $11.2B Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project. A U.S. District Court granted a preliminary injunction preventing the federal government from enforcing a stop-work order after the company argued the halt was causing irreparable financial harm. Dominion has already invested nearly $9B in the project, which is expected to generate enough power for about 600K homes and remains central to its long-term growth strategy.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating and sell-side consensus both stand at Hold [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/D/ratings/quant-ratings].

However, SA Investing Group Leader The Value Portfolio rates the stock a Buy [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4867876-dominion-energy-coastal-wind-moving-forward-with-a-strong-portfolio], citing Dominion’s roughly $50B capital investment plan, a dividend yield near 4.3%, and a long-term EPS growth target of about 6%. With the offshore wind project now about two-thirds complete and data-center-driven electricity demand rising, the company expects stronger cash flow and continued shareholder returns, though project risks and potential equity dilution remain key watch points.

 	* Consensus EPS Estimates: $0.67
 	* Consensus Revenue Estimates: $2.23B
 	* Earnings Insight: The company has beaten EPS estimates in 7 of the past 8 quarters, missing revenue expectations in 5 of those reports.

ALSO REPORTING: ONEOK (OKE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OKE]), Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IIPR]), Diamondback Energy (FANG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FANG]), Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BBBY]), Ovintiv (OVV [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OVV]), Domino's Pizza (DPZ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DPZ]), BioMarin Pharmaceutical (BMRN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BMRN]), and more.

TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 24

HOME DEPOT (HD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HD])

Home Depot (HD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HD]) is set to report Q4 earnings before Tuesday’s market open, with analysts expecting roughly a 16% Y/Y decline in profits alongside a ~4% drop in revenue.

Wall Street maintains a Buy rating on the stock, while Seeking Alpha’s Quant model remains at Hold, reflecting concerns around growth and valuation.

SA author Justin Purohit also takes a neutral stance [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4872410-home-depot-q4-earnings-preview-housing-trends-in-focus-shares-fairly-valued] ahead of the release, noting that shares have held up relatively well despite softness in the housing market, with management’s outlook for housing demand through 2026 likely to be a key focus. He views the stock as fairly valued in the current environment.

By contrast, contributor Vladimir Dimitrov, CFA, retains a Sell rating [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4865053-home-depot-canary-in-the-coal-mine], arguing that recent weakness in the stock may only be the beginning as margins stay under pressure. He also highlights that valuation remains elevated relative to profitability, while macro risks tied to the housing cycle have intensified lately.

 	* Consensus EPS Estimates: $2.53
 	* Consensus Revenue Estimates: $38.03B
 	* Earnings Insight: Home Depot has consistently outperformed revenue estimates in the past 6 of the 8 quarters and EPS forecasts in 50% of those reports.

ALSO REPORTING: Realty Income (O [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/O]), Lucid Group (LCID [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LCID]), AMC Entertainment Holdings (AMC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMC]), American Tower (AMT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMT]), MercadoLibre (MELI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MELI]), HP (HPQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/HPQ]), First Solar (FSLR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FSLR]), EOG Resources (EOG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/EOG]), Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BNS]), Workday (WDAY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/WDAY]), Tanger Factory Outlet Centers (SKT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SKT]), Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CLNE]), Mosaic (MOS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MOS]), LTC Properties (LTC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LTC]), Elanco Animal Health (ELAN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ELAN]), Axon Enterprise (AXON [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AXON]), Range Resources (RRC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RRC]), DigitalOcean (DOCN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DOCN]), Amer Sports (AS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AS]), CAVA Group (CAVA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CAVA]), and more.

WEDNESDAY, FEBRUARY 25

NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA])

NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]) will headline the week’s earnings, with its Q4 report due Wednesday after the close. Analysts expect revenue and earnings to surge roughly 70% Y/Y, driven by sustained AI-chip demand and continued strength in data-center spending.

Recent developments have kept the company in focus, including a broad multi-year AI infrastructure partnership with Meta [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4552879-nvidia-announces-multi-year-partnership-with-meta-to-expand-ai-infrastructure] Platforms (META [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/META]), which expands deployment of Nvidia’s Grace CPUs, Spectrum-X networking, GB300 systems, and confidential computing tools across Meta’s platforms.

Oppenheimer reiterated [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4553985-nvidia-likely-to-offer-typical-upside-when-it-reports-q4-results-oppenheimer] its Outperform rating and $265 price target on NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]), saying the company could deliver a “typical” $2B–$3B revenue upside in Q4, driven by strong demand for GB300 Ultra systems and rising hyperscaler capex, which is projected to climb to about $650B in 2026 from over $400B in 2025.

Analyst Rick Schafer also pointed to Nvidia’s product roadmap as a key growth lever, with the higher-priced Vera Rubin platform expected to ramp later this year and potentially add around $8B in revenue, while expanding AI workloads, rapid growth in reasoning tokens, and a potential $50B opportunity in China could further lift Nvidia’s total addressable market, now estimated near $4T, reinforcing the view that the company remains the dominant platform in AI infrastructure.

Views on NVIDIA (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]) remain sharply split heading into earnings.

While Wall Street broadly holds a Strong Buy stance, Seeking Alpha’s Quant model is more cautious with a Hold [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA/ratings/quant-ratings] on valuation grounds.

Seeking Alpha contributor Dair Sansyzbayev is firmly bullish, calling Nvidia a Strong Buy [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4872717-nvidia-stock-deep-discount-before-earnings] given its dominance in AI accelerators, accelerating revenue and EPS growth, strong operating leverage, and powerful free-cash-flow generation, with hyperscaler spending expected to drive another blowout quarter.

title: Investors Will Want Hawaiian Electric Industries' (NYSE:HE) Growth In ROCE To Persist
content:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. Firstly, we'd want to identify a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and then alongside that, an ever-increasing base of capital employed. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. With that in mind, we've noticed some promising trends at Hawaiian Electric Industries (NYSE:HE) so let's look a bit deeper.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

For those who don't know, ROCE is a measure of a company's yearly pre-tax profit (its return), relative to the capital employed in the business. To calculate this metric for Hawaiian Electric Industries, this is the formula:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.03 = US$225m ÷ (US$8.8b - US$1.4b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Thus, Hawaiian Electric Industries has an ROCE of 3.0%.  In absolute terms, that's a low return and it also under-performs the Electric Utilities industry average of 5.3%.

See our latest analysis for Hawaiian Electric Industries NYSE:HE Return on Capital Employed February 11th 2026

In the above chart we have measured Hawaiian Electric Industries' prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free analyst report for Hawaiian Electric Industries .

How Are Returns Trending?

While the ROCE is still rather low for Hawaiian Electric Industries, we're glad to see it heading in the right direction. The data shows that returns on capital have increased by 31% over the trailing five years. That's a very favorable trend because this means that the company is earning more per dollar of capital that's being employed. Interestingly, the business may be becoming more efficient because it's applying 47% less capital than it was five years ago. If this trend continues, the business might be getting more efficient but it's shrinking in terms of total assets.

For the record though, there was a noticeable increase in the company's current liabilities over the period, so we would attribute some of the ROCE growth to that. The current liabilities has increased to 16% of total assets, so the business is now more funded by the likes of its suppliers or short-term creditors. Keep an eye out for future increases because when the ratio of current liabilities to total assets gets particularly high, this can introduce some new risks for the business.

Story Continues

The Bottom Line

In summary, it's great to see that Hawaiian Electric Industries has been able to turn things around and earn higher returns on lower amounts of capital. And since the stock has fallen 46% over the last five years, there might be an opportunity here. So researching this company further and determining whether or not these trends will continue seems justified.

Like most companies, Hawaiian Electric Industries does come with some risks, and we've found 1 warning sign that you should be aware of.

title: Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (HE): A Bull Case Theory
content:
We came across a bullish thesis on Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. on R. Dennis’s Substack by OppCost. In this article, we will summarize the bulls’ thesis on HE. Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc.'s share was trading at $15.87 as of January 29th. HE’s trailing and forward P/E were 7.28 and 17.36, respectively according to Yahoo Finance.NexGen (NXE) Ends Losing Streak as US Govt Urges Uranium Expansion

ermess/Shutterstock.com

Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, engages in the electric utility business in the United States. A massive institutional “whale” has established a high-conviction bullish position in Hawaiian Electric (HE) through a January 2027 risk-reversal involving 20,000 long $15 calls financed entirely by selling 20,000 $10 puts, creating a synthetic 2 million-share stake at zero net cost. This trade exploits a rare pricing distortion in HE’s volatility surface, where downside protection remains inflated due to lingering fears from the 2023–2024 wildfire crisis, while upside participation trades cheaply.

By monetizing this “distress skew,” the investor captures leveraged upside above $15 and accepts stock only at tangible book value levels, signaling confidence that HE’s downside is largely neutralized following the 2024 global settlement. With liabilities capped and Act 301 enabling securitization of wildfire-related costs, HE’s bankruptcy risk has materially diminished, and settlement opt-out numbers have proven far lower than feared. The balance sheet has stabilized through an equity raise, prefunded settlement payments, and renewed access to the bond market, while management has indicated future funding will avoid the dilutive equity issuance that previously crushed sentiment.

As HE’s rate base expands through mandated wildfire safety investments, book value is set to rise, making the $10 strike increasingly conservative. The whale’s bet targets a return to normalized earnings and a re-rating toward standard utility valuation multiples, with 2027 EPS expected to exceed $1.00 and a 15x P/E supporting a ~$16–$17 price target—placing the calls comfortably in the money. The signal is clear: institutional capital believes HE has bottomed, and the zero-cost risk-reversal offers a superior risk/reward profile versus owning the stock outright.

Previously we covered a bullish thesis on PG&E Corporation (PCG) by Acid Investments in February 2025, which highlighted the market’s exaggerated wildfire concerns despite PCG having no direct liability. The company's stock price has depreciated approximately by 4.53% since our coverage. This is because sentiment briefly shifted toward sector risk. The thesis still stands as fundamentals remain strong. OppCost shares a similar but emphasizes HE’s options-driven “distress skew.”

Story Continues

For a regulated utility, this is a common yardstick because cash generation and rate structures tend to anchor earnings expectations more than rapid revenue swings.

title: Hawaiian Electric Industries to Announce Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Results February 27
content:
HONOLULU, January 21, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (HEI) (NYSE - HE) will announce its fourth quarter and full year 2025 financial results on Friday, February 27 and conduct a webcast and conference call to discuss the results at 11:30 a.m. Hawaii time (4:30 p.m. Eastern time).

To listen to the conference call, dial 1-888-660-6377 (U.S.) or 1-929-203-0797 (international) and enter passcode 2393042. Parties may also access presentation materials and/or listen to the conference call by visiting the conference call link on HEI’s website at www.hei.com under "Investor Relations," sub-heading "News and Events – Events and Presentations."

A replay will be available online and via phone. The online replay will be available on HEI’s website about two hours after the event. An audio replay will also be available about two hours after the event through March 6. To access the audio replay, dial 1-800-770-2030 (U.S.) or 1-647-362-9199 (international) and enter passcode 2393042.

HEI and Hawaiian Electric Company, Inc. (Hawaiian Electric) intend to continue to use HEI’s website, www.hei.com, as a means of disclosing additional information; such disclosures will be included in the Investor Relations section of the website. Accordingly, investors should routinely monitor the Investor Relations section of HEI’s website, in addition to following HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s press releases, HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings and HEI’s public conference calls and webcasts. Investors may sign up to receive e-mail alerts via the Investor Relations section of the website. The information on HEI’s website is not incorporated by reference into this document or into HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s SEC filings unless, and except to the extent, specifically incorporated by reference.

Investors may also wish to refer to the Public Utilities Commission of the State of Hawaii (PUC) website at https://hpuc.my.site.com/cdms/s/ to review documents filed with, and issued by, the PUC. No information on the PUC website is incorporated by reference into this document or into HEI’s and Hawaiian Electric’s SEC filings.

About HEI

HEI provides the energy services that empower much of the economic and community activity of Hawaii. HEI’s electric utility, Hawaiian Electric, supplies power to approximately 95% of Hawaii’s population and is undertaking an ambitious effort to decarbonize its operations and the broader state economy, and modernize and harden the grid to ensure resilience and public safety. For more information, visit www.hei.com.
